packs.name: HashiCorp Vault
packs.description: Protects against destructive Vault CLI operations like deleting secrets, disabling auth/secret engines, revoking leases/tokens, and deleting policies.

structural_patterns.name: vault-secrets-disable
structural_patterns.reason: vault secrets disable disables a secrets engine, causing data loss.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Disabling a secrets engine permanently deletes ALL secrets stored in that engine. Dynamic credentials are revoked immediately. Applications using secrets from this engine will fail authentication. This cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- vault secrets list: Review enabled engines first
- Export secrets before disabling
- Rotate applications to new secrets engine first

structural_patterns.name: vault-kv-destroy
structural_patterns.reason: vault kv destroy permanently deletes secret versions.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Destroy permanently erases secret data including all specified versions. Unlike delete (which is soft-delete in KV v2), destroy is irreversible. The data cannot be recovered even by Vault administrators.

Safer alternatives:
- vault kv delete: Soft-delete with undelete option
- vault kv get: Verify the secret versions first
- Export secret data before destruction

structural_patterns.name: vault-kv-metadata-delete
structural_patterns.reason: vault kv metadata delete removes all versions and metadata for a secret.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting metadata removes ALL versions of a secret and its metadata permanently. This is more destructive than destroy as it removes the entire secret history. The secret path becomes available for reuse.

Safer alternatives:
- vault kv metadata get: Review secret metadata first
- vault kv destroy: Delete specific versions only
- Export all secret versions before deletion

structural_patterns.name: vault-kv-delete
structural_patterns.reason: vault kv delete removes the latest secret version.
structural_patterns.explanation:
In KV v2, delete performs a soft-delete of the latest version. The data can be recovered with undelete until the delete_version_after policy expires. In KV v1, delete is permanent and immediate.

Safer alternatives:
- vault kv get: Verify the secret before deleting
- vault kv undelete: Recover if mistakenly deleted (v2)
- Set appropriate delete_version_after policies

structural_patterns.name: vault-delete
structural_patterns.reason: vault delete removes secrets at a path.
structural_patterns.explanation:
The generic delete command removes data at the specified path. The behavior depends on the secrets engine. For most engines, this is a permanent deletion of the secret data.

Safer alternatives:
- vault read: Verify the path contents first
- Use engine-specific commands for safer operations
- Back up data before deletion

structural_patterns.name: vault-policy-delete
structural_patterns.reason: vault policy delete removes access policies.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a policy immediately affects all tokens with that policy. Users and applications may lose access to secrets they need. Orphaned tokens retain their policy name but lose the permissions.

Safer alternatives:
- vault policy read: Review policy before deletion
- vault token lookup: Check affected tokens
- Create replacement policy before deleting

structural_patterns.name: vault-auth-disable
structural_patterns.reason: vault auth disable disables an auth method.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Disabling an auth method revokes ALL tokens issued by that method and removes all configuration. Users authenticating via this method will immediately lose access. This includes all associated roles and policies.

Safer alternatives:
- vault auth list: Review auth methods first
- Migrate users to another auth method
- Export auth configuration before disabling

structural_patterns.name: vault-token-revoke
structural_patterns.reason: vault token revoke invalidates tokens and can disrupt access.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Revoking a token immediately invalidates it and all child tokens. Applications using this token will fail authentication on next request. With -prefix, all tokens under a prefix tree are revoked.

Safer alternatives:
- vault token lookup: Verify the token before revoking
- Use token TTLs for automatic expiration
- Coordinate token rotation before revocation

structural_patterns.name: vault-lease-revoke
structural_patterns.reason: vault lease revoke invalidates leases and can disrupt access.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Revoking a lease immediately invalidates dynamic credentials (database passwords, AWS keys, etc.). Applications using these credentials will fail. With -prefix, all leases under a path are revoked.

Safer alternatives:
- vault lease lookup: Review lease details first
- Use shorter TTLs for automatic rotation
- Coordinate credential rotation before revocation

packs.name: AWS Secrets Manager
packs.description: Protects against destructive AWS Secrets Manager and SSM Parameter Store operations like delete-secret and delete-parameter.

structural_patterns.name: aws-secretsmanager-delete-secret
structural_patterns.reason: aws secretsmanager delete-secret removes secrets and may cause data loss.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a secret schedules it for deletion (default 30 days) or immediately removes it with --force-delete-without-recovery. Applications using this secret will fail to authenticate or decrypt data.

Safer alternatives:
- aws secretsmanager get-secret-value: Export value first
- aws secretsmanager describe-secret: Check rotation/replication
- Use --recovery-window-in-days for recoverable deletion

structural_patterns.name: aws-secretsmanager-delete-resource-policy
structural_patterns.reason: aws secretsmanager delete-resource-policy removes access controls.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a resource policy removes cross-account access and custom permissions. Other AWS accounts or services that rely on this policy will lose access to the secret immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- aws secretsmanager get-resource-policy: Export policy first
- Verify no cross-account dependencies exist
- Update IAM policies to compensate if needed

structural_patterns.name: aws-secretsmanager-remove-regions
structural_patterns.reason: aws secretsmanager remove-regions-from-replication can reduce availability.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing regions from replication deletes the secret replica in those regions. Applications in removed regions will lose local access to the secret, increasing latency or causing failures during cross-region failover.

Safer alternatives:
- aws secretsmanager describe-secret: Review replica regions
- Verify no applications in target regions use the secret
- Update disaster recovery documentation

structural_patterns.name: aws-secretsmanager-update-secret
structural_patterns.reason: aws secretsmanager update-secret overwrites secret metadata or value.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Updating a secret can change its value, KMS key, or description. The previous value becomes a non-current version. Applications using the old value may fail if automatic rotation is not configured.

Safer alternatives:
- aws secretsmanager get-secret-value: Export current value
- Use put-secret-value to create new version instead
- Test new value in non-production environment first

structural_patterns.name: aws-secretsmanager-put-secret-value
structural_patterns.reason: aws secretsmanager put-secret-value creates a new secret version and can break clients.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Creating a new secret version makes it the current value immediately. Applications caching the old value will use outdated credentials until they refresh. Rotation windows should be coordinated with application deployments.

Safer alternatives:
- aws secretsmanager get-secret-value: Export current value
- Use version staging labels for gradual rollout
- Coordinate secret updates with application deployments

structural_patterns.name: aws-ssm-delete-parameter
structural_patterns.reason: aws ssm delete-parameter removes a parameter and can break deployments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an SSM parameter immediately removes it. Applications, CloudFormation stacks, and deployment scripts referencing this parameter will fail. There is no recovery window like Secrets Manager.

Safer alternatives:
- aws ssm get-parameter: Export value first
- aws ssm describe-parameters: Check parameter history
- Search CloudFormation templates for references

structural_patterns.name: aws-ssm-delete-parameters
structural_patterns.reason: aws ssm delete-parameters removes parameters and can break deployments.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting multiple SSM parameters at once can break numerous applications and deployment pipelines simultaneously. Each parameter is deleted immediately with no recovery option.

Safer alternatives:
- aws ssm get-parameters: Export all values first
- Delete one parameter at a time to limit blast radius
- Verify no active deployments use these parameters

packs.name: 1Password CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive 1Password CLI operations like deleting items, documents, users, groups, and vaults.

structural_patterns.name: op-item-delete
structural_patterns.reason: op item delete removes secret items (including archive operations).
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a 1Password item permanently removes the secret (passwords, API keys, credentials). Without --archive, this cannot be undone. Applications and users relying on this item will lose access to the stored credentials.

Safer alternatives:
- op item get: Export item fields before deletion
- op item delete --archive: Move to archive for recovery
- Verify no applications reference this item

structural_patterns.name: op-document-delete
structural_patterns.reason: op document delete removes secure documents (including archive operations).
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a 1Password document permanently removes the file (certificates, keys, configurations). Unlike items, documents may contain unique binary data that cannot be recreated from memory.

Safer alternatives:
- op document get: Download document before deletion
- op document delete --archive: Move to archive for recovery
- Back up to secure local storage first

structural_patterns.name: op-vault-delete
structural_patterns.reason: op vault delete removes an entire vault.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a vault removes all items, documents, and permissions within it. This affects all users with vault access and any applications using items from this vault. This action cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- op item list --vault=X: Inventory vault contents
- Export items and documents before deletion
- Move items to a different vault instead

structural_patterns.name: op-user-delete
structural_patterns.reason: op user delete removes a user from 1Password.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a user revokes their access to all vaults and items. Any private vaults owned by the user may be transferred or lost. Service accounts and integrations created by this user may also be affected.

Safer alternatives:
- op user suspend: Suspend instead of delete for recovery
- op user get: Document user permissions first
- Transfer vault ownership before deletion

structural_patterns.name: op-group-delete
structural_patterns.reason: op group delete removes a group.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a group removes the permission boundary and all vault assignments. Members lose access to vaults shared through this group. This affects team organization and access control policies.

Safer alternatives:
- op group list: Review group membership first
- Reassign vault permissions to individual users
- Create replacement group before deleting old one

structural_patterns.name: op-connect-token-delete
structural_patterns.reason: op connect token delete revokes access tokens.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Connect token immediately revokes API access for integrations using it. CI/CD pipelines, deployment scripts, and applications fetching secrets will fail to authenticate until a new token is configured.

Safer alternatives:
- op connect token list: Document token usage first
- Create new token before deleting old one
- Update integrations with new token prior to deletion

packs.name: Doppler CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive Doppler CLI operations like deleting secrets, configs, environments, or projects.

structural_patterns.name: doppler-secrets-delete
structural_patterns.reason: doppler secrets delete removes secrets.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a secret removes configuration data that applications depend on. Services using 'doppler run' or synced secrets will fail when the secret is not found. Deleted secrets cannot be recovered.

Safer alternatives:
- doppler secrets get: Export secret value first
- doppler secrets set: Update value instead of deleting
- Verify which environments/configs reference the secret

structural_patterns.name: doppler-projects-delete
structural_patterns.reason: doppler projects delete removes a project.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Doppler project removes all environments, configs, and secrets within it. All applications referencing this project will lose access to their secrets. This action cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- doppler secrets download: Export all secrets first
- Review active integrations and service tokens
- Archive the project configuration for recovery

structural_patterns.name: doppler-environments-delete
structural_patterns.reason: doppler environments delete removes an environment.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Doppler environment removes all configs within that environment. Applications targeting this environment (dev, staging, prod) will fail to retrieve secrets. Inherited configurations are also lost.

Safer alternatives:
- doppler secrets download: Export environment secrets
- doppler configs list: Review all configs in environment
- Ensure no active deployments use this environment

structural_patterns.name: doppler-configs-delete
structural_patterns.reason: doppler configs delete removes a config.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Doppler config removes all secret values specific to that config. Branch configs and inheriting configs may also be affected. Applications using this config will fail to start or authenticate.

Safer alternatives:
- doppler secrets download --config=X: Export config secrets
- doppler configs list: Understand config hierarchy
- Migrate secrets to a new config before deletion

packs.name: Bitwarden CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive Bitwarden CLI operations like deleting, editing, or moving items.

structural_patterns.name: bw-delete
structural_patterns.reason: bw delete permanently removes items from the Bitwarden vault.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Bitwarden item permanently removes passwords, notes, or other secrets from the vault. Without the --permanent flag, items go to the trash but can still be lost if trash is emptied. Shared items affect all organization members.

Safer alternatives:
- bw get: Export the item data before deletion
- bw list: Review items to confirm the right target
- Move the item to a different collection instead

structural_patterns.name: bw-edit
structural_patterns.reason: bw edit modifies existing vault items and can overwrite credentials.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Editing a Bitwarden item overwrites its fields (password, username, notes, URIs). The previous values are not versioned and cannot be recovered. Shared items are modified for all organization members simultaneously.

Safer alternatives:
- bw get: Export item data before editing
- Create a new item with updated values
- Verify the item ID before editing

structural_patterns.name: bw-move
structural_patterns.reason: bw move changes item organization membership and can affect access.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Moving an item between organizations changes who has access to the secret. Members of the source organization lose access while members of the target organization gain it. This can break workflows that depend on the item's current location.

Safer alternatives:
- bw get: Review the item before moving
- bw list: Confirm organization memberships
- Share the item rather than moving it

packs.name: GCP Secret Manager
packs.description: Protects against destructive GCP Secret Manager operations like deleting secrets, destroying or disabling versions, and modifying IAM policies.

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-secrets-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud secrets delete permanently removes a secret and all its versions.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a GCP secret permanently removes the secret and all of its versions. Applications using this secret via Secret Manager API will fail immediately. This action cannot be undone.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud secrets versions access: Export the secret value first
- gcloud secrets describe: Review secret metadata
- Disable individual versions instead of deleting the entire secret

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-secrets-versions-destroy
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud secrets versions destroy permanently destroys a secret version's data.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Destroying a secret version permanently erases its payload data. The version metadata remains but the secret value cannot be recovered. Applications pinned to this version will fail.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud secrets versions access: Export the value first
- gcloud secrets versions disable: Disable instead of destroy
- Verify no workloads reference this specific version

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-secrets-versions-disable
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud secrets versions disable prevents access to a secret version.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Disabling a secret version prevents any access to its data. Applications requesting this version will receive an error. Unlike destroy, this is reversible with 'versions enable'.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud secrets versions describe: Check version state first
- Verify no active workloads use this version
- gcloud secrets versions enable: Re-enable if disabled by mistake

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-secrets-update
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud secrets update modifies secret metadata including replication and expiration.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Updating a secret can change its labels, replication policy, expiration, and rotation settings. Changing replication may affect availability in certain regions. Modifying expiration can cause unexpected secret deletion.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud secrets describe: Review current metadata first
- Test changes in a non-production project
- Update labels separately from replication settings

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-secrets-set-iam-policy
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud secrets set-iam-policy replaces the entire IAM policy on a secret.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Setting an IAM policy replaces the entire access control list for a secret. Missing entries in the new policy will revoke access for those principals. Applications and service accounts may lose access immediately.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud secrets get-iam-policy: Export current policy first
- Use add-iam-policy-binding for incremental changes
- Diff the new policy against the current one before applying

structural_patterns.name: gcloud-secrets-remove-iam-policy-binding
structural_patterns.reason: gcloud secrets remove-iam-policy-binding revokes a principal's access to a secret.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing an IAM policy binding revokes a specific principal's access to the secret. If the principal is a service account used by production workloads, those workloads will fail to access the secret.

Safer alternatives:
- gcloud secrets get-iam-policy: Review current bindings
- Verify the principal is not used by active workloads
- Add a replacement binding before removing the old one

packs.name: Azure Key Vault
packs.description: Protects against destructive Azure Key Vault operations on secrets, keys, and certificates including delete and purge.

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-secret-delete
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault secret delete removes a secret from Key Vault.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a Key Vault secret soft-deletes it by default. Applications using this secret will fail immediately. If purge protection is not enabled, the secret can be permanently lost.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault secret show: Export the secret value first
- az keyvault secret list-versions: Review all versions
- Verify purge protection is enabled on the vault

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-secret-purge
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault secret purge permanently destroys a deleted secret.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Purging a secret permanently destroys it with no possibility of recovery. This bypasses the soft-delete retention period. The secret data is irrecoverably lost.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault secret list-deleted: Review deleted secrets first
- az keyvault secret recover: Recover instead of purge
- Let the soft-delete retention period expire naturally

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-key-delete
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault key delete removes an encryption key from Key Vault.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an encryption key can render encrypted data permanently inaccessible. Applications using this key for encryption/decryption, signing, or wrapping will fail. Disk encryption keys being deleted can lock out entire VMs.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault key show: Verify the key before deletion
- az keyvault key list-versions: Review key versions
- Disable the key instead of deleting it

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-key-purge
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault key purge permanently destroys a deleted key.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Purging a key permanently destroys it. Any data encrypted with this key becomes permanently inaccessible. This includes Azure Disk Encryption, Storage Service Encryption, and application-level encryption.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault key list-deleted: Review deleted keys first
- az keyvault key recover: Recover instead of purge
- Verify no encrypted resources depend on this key

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-certificate-delete
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault certificate delete removes a certificate from Key Vault.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting a certificate removes both the certificate and its private key. TLS endpoints using this certificate will fail. Automated renewal is also stopped.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault certificate show: Review certificate details
- az keyvault certificate list: Inventory certificates
- Disable the certificate instead of deleting it

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-certificate-purge
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault certificate purge permanently destroys a deleted certificate.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Purging a certificate permanently destroys it and its private key. TLS configurations depending on this certificate cannot be restored. Re-issuance from the CA may be required.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault certificate list-deleted: Review deleted certs
- az keyvault certificate recover: Recover instead of purge
- Let the soft-delete retention period expire naturally

structural_patterns.name: az-keyvault-purge
structural_patterns.reason: az keyvault purge permanently destroys a deleted vault and all its contents.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Purging an entire Key Vault permanently destroys the vault and all secrets, keys, and certificates within it. This is the most destructive Key Vault operation. All applications using any resource from this vault will fail.

Safer alternatives:
- az keyvault list-deleted: Review deleted vaults first
- az keyvault recover: Recover the vault instead
- Export all resources before purging

packs.name: LastPass CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive LastPass CLI operations like removing, editing, or moving items.

structural_patterns.name: lpass-rm
structural_patterns.reason: lpass rm permanently removes items from the LastPass vault.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a LastPass item permanently deletes passwords, secure notes, or other credentials. The item is moved to the trash but can be permanently lost if trash is emptied. Shared items affect all shared-folder members.

Safer alternatives:
- lpass show: Export the item data before removal
- lpass ls: Verify the correct item path
- Move the item to a different folder instead

structural_patterns.name: lpass-edit
structural_patterns.reason: lpass edit modifies existing vault items and can overwrite credentials.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Editing a LastPass item overwrites its fields. Previous values are not versioned and cannot be recovered. Shared folder items are modified for all members.

Safer alternatives:
- lpass show: Export item data before editing
- lpass duplicate: Create a copy before modifying
- Verify the item ID or path before editing

structural_patterns.name: lpass-mv
structural_patterns.reason: lpass mv moves items between folders and can change access permissions.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Moving a LastPass item between folders can change who has access to it. Moving from a shared folder to a personal folder removes access for other shared-folder members.

Safer alternatives:
- lpass show: Review the item before moving
- lpass ls: Confirm the source and target folder paths
- Duplicate the item instead of moving

packs.name: Infisical CLI
packs.description: Protects against destructive Infisical CLI operations like deleting or overwriting secrets.

structural_patterns.name: infisical-secrets-delete
structural_patterns.reason: infisical secrets delete removes secrets from the project.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Deleting an Infisical secret removes it from the specified environment. Applications using 'infisical run' to inject this secret will fail at startup. The deletion affects the selected environment and workspace.

Safer alternatives:
- infisical secrets get: Export the secret value first
- infisical export: Export all secrets before deletion
- Verify which environments reference the secret

structural_patterns.name: infisical-secrets-set
structural_patterns.reason: infisical secrets set creates or overwrites secret values.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Setting a secret overwrites its current value in the specified environment. Applications relying on the previous value may break. Secret overrides and references may also be affected.

Safer alternatives:
- infisical secrets get: Export current value first
- infisical export: Back up all secrets before changes
- Test in a development environment first

packs.name: Unix Pass
packs.description: Protects against destructive operations in the standard Unix password manager (pass).

structural_patterns.name: pass-rm
structural_patterns.reason: pass rm permanently removes password entries from the store.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a pass entry deletes the GPG-encrypted password file. With -r or --recursive, entire directories of passwords are removed. The git history may retain the data but accessing it requires manual recovery.

Safer alternatives:
- pass show: Export the password before removal
- pass git log: Verify the entry exists and review history
- pass cp: Copy to a backup location first

structural_patterns.name: pass-mv
structural_patterns.reason: pass mv renames or moves password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Moving a pass entry changes its path in the password store. Scripts and aliases referencing the old path will fail to retrieve the password. If the destination already exists, it may be overwritten.

Safer alternatives:
- pass show: Export the password before moving
- pass cp: Copy instead of move to preserve the original
- Verify the destination path does not exist

structural_patterns.name: pass-edit
structural_patterns.reason: pass edit modifies password entries in place.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Editing a pass entry opens it for modification. The previous content is overwritten with no built-in undo. While git history may retain the old version, recovery requires manual git operations.

Safer alternatives:
- pass show: Export current content before editing
- pass git log: Verify the entry's history
- pass cp: Create a backup copy before editing

structural_patterns.name: pass-insert
structural_patterns.reason: pass insert creates or overwrites password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Inserting a password creates a new entry or overwrites an existing one with --force. If the entry already exists and is overwritten, the previous password is lost unless recoverable from git history.

Safer alternatives:
- pass show: Check if the entry already exists
- pass ls: Review the store structure
- Use pass generate for random passwords instead

structural_patterns.name: pass-generate
structural_patterns.reason: pass generate creates or overwrites passwords with random values.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Generating a password replaces the existing entry with a new random value. Applications and services using the old password will fail authentication. With --in-place, only the first line is replaced.

Safer alternatives:
- pass show: Export the current password before generating
- Use --no-symbols or --clip to preview first
- Coordinate password rotation with dependent services

packs.name: Gopass
packs.description: Protects against destructive gopass operations like removing, moving, editing, or unmounting stores.

structural_patterns.name: gopass-rm
structural_patterns.reason: gopass rm permanently removes password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a gopass entry deletes the encrypted password file. With -r or --recursive, entire subtrees of passwords are removed. The git history may retain the data but recovery requires manual git operations.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Export the secret before removal
- gopass ls: Verify the entry path
- gopass cp: Copy to a backup location first

structural_patterns.name: gopass-delete
structural_patterns.reason: gopass delete permanently removes password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Delete is an alias for rm in gopass. It permanently removes the encrypted password file. The same risks and alternatives apply as with gopass rm.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Export the secret before deletion
- gopass ls: Verify the entry path
- gopass cp: Copy to a backup location first

structural_patterns.name: gopass-mv
structural_patterns.reason: gopass mv renames or moves password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Moving a gopass entry changes its path in the store. Scripts and tools referencing the old path will fail. If moving between mounted stores, access permissions may change.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Export the secret before moving
- gopass cp: Copy instead of move to preserve the original
- Verify the destination path does not exist

structural_patterns.name: gopass-move
structural_patterns.reason: gopass move renames or moves password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Move is an alias for mv in gopass. It changes the entry's path in the store. The same risks and alternatives apply as with gopass mv.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Export the secret before moving
- gopass cp: Copy instead of move
- Verify the destination path does not exist

structural_patterns.name: gopass-edit
structural_patterns.reason: gopass edit modifies password entries in place.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Editing a gopass entry opens it for modification. The previous content is overwritten. While git history may retain the old version, recovery requires manual git operations.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Export current content before editing
- gopass cat: View the raw content first
- gopass cp: Create a backup copy before editing

structural_patterns.name: gopass-insert
structural_patterns.reason: gopass insert creates or overwrites password entries.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Inserting a secret creates a new entry or overwrites an existing one. If the entry exists, the previous value is replaced. Recovery requires manual git operations.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Check if the entry already exists
- gopass ls: Review the store structure
- Use gopass generate for random passwords instead

structural_patterns.name: gopass-generate
structural_patterns.reason: gopass generate creates or overwrites passwords with random values.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Generating a password replaces the existing entry with a new random value. Services using the old password will fail authentication until updated.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass show: Export the current password first
- Coordinate password rotation with dependent services
- Use --force only when intentional

structural_patterns.name: gopass-mounts-remove
structural_patterns.reason: gopass mounts remove unmounts a password store.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Removing a mount detaches a sub-store from gopass. The passwords in the unmounted store become inaccessible through gopass. The underlying data is preserved but workflows depending on the mount path will break.

Safer alternatives:
- gopass mounts: Review current mounts first
- gopass ls mountname/: Inventory the mount's contents
- Document the mount path for re-mounting

packs.name: CyberArk Conjur
packs.description: Protects against destructive Conjur CLI operations like setting variables, loading policies, and rotating API keys.

structural_patterns.name: conjur-variable-set
structural_patterns.reason: conjur variable set overwrites a secret variable's value.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Setting a Conjur variable overwrites its current value. Applications fetching this variable will receive the new value immediately. The previous value is retained in the audit log but not easily recoverable.

Safer alternatives:
- conjur variable get: Export the current value first
- conjur variable value: Verify the current value
- Test the new value in a non-production environment

structural_patterns.name: conjur-policy-load
structural_patterns.reason: conjur policy load modifies access control and can delete resources.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Loading a Conjur policy can create, update, or delete resources, roles, and permissions. With --replace, existing policy is completely replaced, removing any resources not in the new policy. This can revoke access for applications and users.

Safer alternatives:
- conjur list: Review current resources before loading
- Use --append mode for additive changes
- Diff the new policy against the current one
- Test policy in a non-production environment first

structural_patterns.name: conjur-host-rotate-api-key
structural_patterns.reason: conjur host rotate-api-key invalidates a host's authentication credentials.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Rotating a host API key immediately invalidates the current key. The host (application, service) cannot authenticate until it receives the new key. Automated deployments and running services will fail to fetch secrets.

Safer alternatives:
- conjur list: Identify affected hosts
- Coordinate key rotation with deployment pipelines
- Update the host's stored API key before rotating

structural_patterns.name: conjur-user-rotate-api-key
structural_patterns.reason: conjur user rotate-api-key invalidates a user's authentication credentials.
structural_patterns.explanation:
Rotating a user API key immediately invalidates their current key. The user cannot authenticate until they receive the new key. Service accounts and automation using this user's credentials will fail.

Safer alternatives:
- conjur whoami: Verify which user is targeted
- Coordinate rotation with the affected user
- Update stored credentials before rotating
